perturbation.original_text: 1.1 For the purposes of this Agreement, Confidential Information means any data or proprietary information of the Discloser that is not generally known to the public or has not yet been revealed, whether in tangible or intangible form, whenever and however disclosed, including, but not limited to: (i) any scientific or technical information, invention, d e s i g n , p r o c e s s , procedure, formula, improvement, technology or method; (ii) any concepts, samples, reports, data, know-how, works-in-progress, designs, drawings, photographs, development tools, s p e c i f i c a t i o n s , software programs, source code, object code, flow charts, and databases; (iii) any marketing strategies, plans, financial information, or projections, operations, sales estimates, business plans and performance results relating European IPR Helpdesk | Page 4 of 8 to the Discloser’s past, present or future business activities, or those of its affiliates, subsidiaries and affiliated companies; (iv) trade secrets; plans for products or services, and customer or supplier lists; (v) any other information that should reasonably be recognized as Confidential Information by the Discloser.
perturbation.changed_text: 1.1 For the purposes of this Agreement, Confidential Information means any data or proprietary information of the Discloser that is not generally known to the public or has not yet been revealed, whether in tangible or intangible form, whenever and however disclosed, including, but not limited to: (i) any scientific or technical information, invention, d e s i g n , p r o c e s s , procedure, formula, improvement, technology or method; (ii) any concepts, samples, reports, data, know-how, works-in-progress, designs, drawings, photographs, development tools, s p e c i f i c a t i o n s , software programs, source code, object code, flow charts, and databases; (iii) any marketing strategies, plans, financial information, or projections, operations, sales estimates, business plans and performance results relating European IPR Helpdesk | Page 4 of 8 to the Discloser’s past, present or future business activities, or those of its affiliates, subsidiaries and affiliated companies; (iv) trade secrets as solely determined by the Recipient; plans for products or services, and customer or supplier lists; (v) any other information that should reasonably be recognized as Confidential Information by the Discloser.
perturbation.explanation: Modifying the definition of 'trade secrets' to be solely determined by the Recipient introduces a conflict. The legal definition of a trade secret requires specific characteristics to be met, it cannot be unilaterally decided.
perturbation.contradicted_law: The Uniform Trade Secrets Act (UTSA) defines trade secrets based on specific criteria, not subjective determination by a recipient.
perturbation.law_explanation: The UTSA defines a 'trade secret' as information that derives independent economic value from not being generally known or readily ascertainable, and is subject to reasonable efforts to maintain its secrecy. Giving the Recipient sole discretion contradicts this, as they could arbitrarily deem something a trade secret even if it doesn't meet the legal criteria.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 404 | Berkman Klein Center Skip to the main content Page Cannot Be Found Brave traveler, your journey on our site has brought you to an abrupt dead end. Perhaps you wish to explore one of these other options? About Us Get Involved Events Community Publications Projects & Tools Last updated Mar 27, 2024
perturbation.justification: The modification allows the Recipient to solely determine what constitutes a 'trade secret,' conflicting with the established legal definition that requires specific characteristics beyond the Recipient's subjective determination. The Law Explanation corroborates that the UTSA requires trade secrets to derive independent economic value and be subject to reasonable secrecy efforts. By granting the Recipient unilateral power, the modified contract bypasses these legal requirements, thus creating a clear legal contradiction. The lack of relevant scraped snippets does not diminish this contradiction, as the Law Explanation provides a valid legal basis, and the Perturbation Explanation details how the modified text undermines established legal principles.

perturbation.original_text: 1.5 Notwithstanding the aforementioned Confidential I n f o r m a t i o n s h a l l e x c l u d e information that: (i) is already in the public domain at the time of disclosure by the Discloser to the Recipient or thereafter enters the public domain without any breach of the terms of this Agreement; (ii) was already known by the Recipient before the m o m e n t o f d i s c l o s u r e (under evidence of reasonable proof or written record of such disclosure); (iii) is subsequently communicated to the Recipient without any obligation of confidence from a third party who is in lawful possession thereof and under no obligation of confidence to the Discloser; (iv) becomes publicly available by other means than a breach of the confidentiality obligations by the Recipient (not through fault or failure to act by the Recipient); (iv) is or has been developed independently by employees, consultants or agents of the Recipient (proved by reasonable means) without violation of the European IPR Helpdesk | Page 5 of 8 terms of this Agreement or reference or access to any Confidential Information pertaining to the Discloser.
perturbation.changed_text: 1.5 Notwithstanding the aforementioned Confidential I n f o r m a t i o n s h a l l e x c l u d e information that: (i) is already in the public domain at the time of disclosure by the Discloser to the Recipient or thereafter enters the public domain without any breach of the terms of this Agreement; (ii) was already known by the Recipient before the m o m e n t o f d i s c l o s u r e (without any need for evidence or written record of such disclosure); (iii) is subsequently communicated to the Recipient without any obligation of confidence from a third party who is in lawful possession thereof and under no obligation of confidence to the Discloser; (iv) becomes publicly available by other means than a breach of the confidentiality obligations by the Recipient (not through fault or failure to act by the Recipient); (iv) is or has been developed independently by employees, consultants or agents of the Recipient (proved by reasonable means) without violation of the European IPR Helpdesk | Page 5 of 8 terms of this Agreement or reference or access to any Confidential Information pertaining to the Discloser.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the requirement for 'evidence of reasonable proof or written record' when claiming prior knowledge weakens the protection of confidential information. It contradicts the principle that the party claiming prior knowledge bears the burden of proof.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Legal standards generally require evidence to support claims, particularly in contract disputes.
perturbation.law_explanation: FRE 301 addresses presumptions in civil cases and states that the party against whom a presumption is directed has the burden of producing evidence to rebut the presumption. Removing the need for evidence of prior knowledge shifts the burden of proof unfairly onto the Discloser and contradicts established legal principles.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Rule 301. Presumptions in Civil Cases Generally | Federal Rules of Evidence | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Rule 301. Presumptions in Civil Cases Generally In a civil case, unless a federal statute or these rules provide otherwise, the party against whom a presumption is directed has the burden of producing evidence to rebut the presumption. But this rule does not shift the burden of persuasion, which remains on the party who had it originally. Notes (Pub. L. 93–595, §1, Jan. 2, 1975, 88 Stat. 1931; Apr. 26, 2011, eff. Dec. 1, 2011.) Notes of Advisory Committee on Proposed Rules This rule governs presumptions generally. See Rule 302 for presumptions controlled by state law and Rule 303 [deleted] for those against an accused in a criminal case. Presumptions governed by this rule are given the effect of placing upon the opposing party the burden of establishing the nonexistence of the presumed fact, once the party invoking the presumption establishes the basic facts giving rise to it. The same considerations of fairness, policy, and probability which dictate the allocation of the burden of the various elements of a case as between the prima facie case of a plaintiff and affirmative defenses also underlie the creation of presumptions. These considerations are not satisfied by giving a lesser effect to presumptions. Morgan and Maguire, Looking Backward and Forward at Evidence, 50 Harv.L.Rev. 909, 913 (1937); Morgan, Instructing the Jury upon Presumptions and Burdon of Proof, 47 Harv.L.Rev. 59, 82 1933); Cleary, Presuming and Pleading: An Essay on Juristic Immaturity, 12 Stan.L.Rev. 5 (1959). The so-called “bursting bubble” theory, under which a presumption vanishes upon the introduction of evidence which would support a finding of the nonexistence of the presumed fact, even though not believed, is rejected as according presumptions too “slight and evanescent” an effect. Morgan and Maguire, supra , at p. 913. In the op
perturbation.justification: The modified text removes the requirement for 'evidence of reasonable proof or written record' when claiming prior knowledge. FRE 301 states that 'the party against whom a presumption is directed has the burden of producing evidence to rebut the presumption.' By removing the evidentiary requirement, the changed text shifts the burden of proof from the Recipient (claiming prior knowledge) to the Discloser, directly contradicting FRE 301, which places the burden of producing evidence on the party opposing the presumption. The scraped law snippet reinforces this principle, stating that the opposing party has the burden of establishing the nonexistence of the presumed fact.

perturbation.original_text: 3.10 The Recipient agrees that the Discloser will suffer irreparable damage if its Confidential Information is made public, released to a third party, or otherwise disclosed in breach of this Agreement and that the Discloser shall be entitled to obtain injunctive relief against a threatened breach or continuation of any such a breach and, in the event of such breach, an award of actual and exemplary damages from any court of competent jurisdiction.
perturbation.changed_text: 3.10 The Recipient agrees that the Discloser will suffer irreparable damage if its Confidential Information is made public, released to a third party, or otherwise disclosed in breach of this Agreement and that the Discloser shall be entitled to obtain injunctive relief against a threatened breach or continuation of any such a breach and, in the event of such breach, an award of only nominal damages from any court of competent jurisdiction.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'actual and exemplary damages' to 'only nominal damages' significantly limits the Discloser's potential remedy for a breach of confidentiality. This contradicts the legal principle of making the injured party whole.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Contract law generally aims to compensate the injured party for losses resulting from a breach.
perturbation.law_explanation: Restatement (Second) of Contracts § 344 outlines the purpose of remedies as protecting the injured party's expectation interest, reliance interest, and restitution interest. Limiting damages to nominal amounts undermines the goal of compensating the Discloser for actual losses suffered due to the breach, conflicting with established contract law principles.

perturbation.justification: The original contract allows for 'actual and exemplary damages,' while the changed version limits damages to 'only nominal damages.' The Law Explanation states this undermines compensating the Discloser for actual losses suffered due to the breach, conflicting with contract law principles as remedies should protect the injured party's expectation interest, reliance interest, and restitution interest. Scraped law snippets are not relevant in this case as it is not complete, therefore, this determination relies on the original and changed text and the Law Explanation provided.

perturbation.original_text: 4.2 Applicable Law and Jurisdiction This Agreement shall be construed and interpreted by the laws of [choose the applicable law]. The court of [choose the jurisdiction to settle disputes] shall have jurisdiction.
perturbation.changed_text: 4.2 Applicable Law and Jurisdiction This Agreement shall be construed and interpreted by the laws of [choose the applicable law]. Disputes shall be resolved through mandatory interpretive dance, judged by a panel of randomly selected passersby.
perturbation.explanation: Replacing a court of law with "mandatory interpretive dance" for dispute resolution directly contradicts established legal norms and jurisdictional principles.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Enforcement of contracts generally requires a recognized legal forum.
perturbation.law_explanation: Article III, Section 1 of the U.S. Constitution vests the judicial power of the United States in the Supreme Court and inferior courts established by Congress. It implies that dispute resolution must be adjudicated by a court. Substituting this with "interpretive dance" is clearly not a legal form of dispute resolution.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Article III | U.S. Constitution | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Article III Primary tabs Section 1. The judicial power of the United States, shall be vested in one Supreme Court, and in such inferior courts as the Congress may from time to time ordain and establish. The judges, both of the supreme and inferior courts, shall hold their offices during good behaviour, and shall, at stated times, receive for their services, a compensation, which shall not be diminished during their continuance in office. Section 2. The judicial power shall extend to all cases, in law and equity, arising under this Constitution, the laws of the United States, and treaties made, or which shall be made, under their authority;--to all cases affecting ambassadors, other public ministers and consuls;--to all cases of admiralty and maritime jurisdiction;--to controversies to which the United States shall be a party;--to controversies between two or more states;-- between a state and citizens of another state ;--between citizens of different states;--between citizens of the same state claiming lands under grants of different states, and between a state, or the citizens thereof, and foreign states, citizens or subjects. In all cases affecting ambassadors, other public ministers and consuls, and those in which a state shall be party, the Supreme Court shall have original jurisdiction. In all the other cases before mentioned, the Supreme Court shall have appellate jurisdiction, both as to law and fact, with such exceptions, and under such regulations as the Congress shall make. The trial of all crimes, except in cases of impeachment, shall be by jury; and such trial shall be held in the state where the said crimes shall have been committed; but when not committed within any state, the trial shall be at such place or places as the Congress may by law have directed. Section 3. Treason against the United States, shall consist only in levying war against them, or in adhering to their enemies, giving them aid and comfort. No person shall be convicted of treason unless on the testimony of two witnes
perturbation.justification: The changed text replaces a court of law with "mandatory interpretive dance" for dispute resolution. This directly contradicts the U.S. Constitution, Article III, Section 1, which states that "The judicial power of the United States, shall be vested in one Supreme Court, and in such inferior courts as the Congress may from time to time ordain and establish." The modification removes the legally established dispute resolution system with a non-legal one, making it a clear violation of the law. The Law Explanation and Perturbation Explanation support this assessment.
